report:
### TLDR
The developer focused on debugging and integrating a new agent tracing system for Python in Meridiona/meridian, resolving import errors and fixing missing dependencies within virtual environments. They were investigating connectivity issues between MLX embedding services, Qwen models, and the Tauri dev server to implement automated activity logging using bash scripts without writing custom code.

### Core Tasks & Projects
**Agent Pipeline Debugging**. The developer investigated `/worklog_pipeline` where imports like `WORKLOG_SYSTEM` caused 500 Internal Server Errors during runtime checks. They resolved this by re-syncing the venv and adding `openinference-instrumentation-agno`, which fixed import errors in classification scripts allowing runs to proceed. This took most of an hour as they debugged logs showing failed trace imports, requiring a new pipeline script instead of manual recompilation to ensure production readiness with minimal downtime.
(Why: Missing imports prevented task matching workflows from running during user sessions) (How: Re-synced venv and added missing instrument path settings in PyrightConfig).

**Observability & Tooling Setup**. The developer consulted documentation at docs.ag3.com regarding OpenTelemetry and Trace visualization for the Meridian project. They decided to configure `setup_agno_tracing` as a patch rather than installing multiple separate observation agents manually via Tauri or Python extensions. This decision allowed them to trace agent runs automatically through an MLX server process without recompiling the full application code, which saved significant development time while ensuring logs were written to the Meridian dashboard correctly.
(Time: ~30 mins) (How: Used bash script and patching imports instead of modifying the agent definition file directly).

### Key Decisions
* "**The developer chose to add a missing `openinference` import**" because installing it via extra flags solved trace visibility without needing complex environment setup changes that would impact other services. The decision was made because checking logs revealed missing instrumentation for specific Python modules requiring manual fixes in the codebase, which reduced debugging time significantly.

### Resources Consulted
*   docs.agno.com/docs/trace vs OpenObserve documentation pages (SDK Agent Ops integration)
*   Meridiona/meridian repos and terminal session logs (venv sync commands, `run-match-tier1.sh`)
*   OpenTelemetry support resources and MLX server setup guides (via Chrome browser search snippets found in history tabs).

## Coding sessions (agent summaries — verbatim)

[07:31 · Claude Code]
The developer fixed a VS Code Python import navigation issue by configuring the workspace to use the correct interpreter and Pyright settings. They then cleaned up observability code by removing all OpenTelemetry spans and logging from five pipeline stages. The developer refactored the tier-1 task classifier to use agno's idiomatic `instructions=` constructor parameter instead of post-construction assignment, simplified the output schema by removing the per-match `why` field, created a new `run-match-tier1.sh` script for isolated testing, and initiated a background refactoring to rename the matching concept to "task classification" throughout the codebase.

[07:35 · Claude Code]
The developer completed the code review cycle for PR #362. After removing the dead `is_version_label` function, the developer committed nine files of review fixes, posted replies to all 12 GitHub review comments explaining what was fixed and why some items were left as-is, handled failing tests by removing test cases that relied on the removed version-label detection, and addressed the final low-priority item by promoting `local_hour_start_utc` from a private indexer function into `meridian_core::date` as a shared public utility. All tests pass and changes are pushed to `feat/proposed-ticket-approval`.

[07:41 · Claude Code]
The developer performed a security audit of seven files in the Meridian project to verify they don't contain high-severity vulnerabilities. The audit targeted common attack vectors: SQL injection, command injection, path traversal, hardcoded credentials, missing input validation at API boundaries, and unsafe subprocess calls with shell=True or unvalidated exec paths.

The developer read each file sequentially—observability.py, session_distiller.py, claude.rs, config.rs, session_builder.rs, post.rs, and WorklogsView.tsx—examining how they handle user input, construct database queries, invoke external processes, and manage file paths and environment variables. The review confirmed that observability.py sanitizes agent names to prevent path traversal, session_distiller.py uses parameterized SQL queries with placeholders, claude.rs invokes the claude command with hardcoded arguments and drops sensitive environment variables from the child process, config.rs safely expands paths without credential exposure, and post.rs uses parameterized queries through sqlx. The audit found no HIGH or CRITICAL severity vulnerabilities across any of the files and reported the result as NO_HIGH_FINDINGS.

[07:44 · Claude Code]
The developer performed a security audit of two files in the Meridian project—`src/etl/session_builder.rs` and `tests/etl_coding_agent_skip.rs`—to identify high and critical severity vulnerabilities including SQL injection, command injection, path traversal, hardcoded credentials, missing input validation at API boundaries, and unsafe subprocess calls. After reading and analyzing both files, the developer found no high or critical severity findings. The codebase demonstrated secure practices: JSON deserialization used defensive error handling with `.unwrap_or_default()` patterns, no string interpolation into SQL queries, parameterized database operations using `.bind()` methods, absence of subprocess calls, and input validation through RFC3339 timestamp parsing. The audit confirmed the changes were safe to proceed with.

[07:58 · Claude Code]
The developer conducted a security audit of two Rust files in the Meridian project—`meridian-core/src/util/date.rs` and `src/coding_agent_session_ingest/indexer.rs`—checking for HIGH and CRITICAL severity security issues (SQL injection, command injection, path traversal, hardcoded credentials, missing input validation, unsafe subprocess calls). The developer read both files and evaluated them systematically: `date.rs` contains pure date math with no attack surface; `indexer.rs` uses parameterized SQL queries via sqlx, safely iterates directories without user-constructed paths, spawns a hardcoded `ps` command with fixed arguments, and safely expands environment variables. No HIGH or CRITICAL severity findings were identified in either file. Result: NO_HIGH_FINDINGS.

[07:59 · Claude Code]
The developer verified the current agno observability setup and added tracing infrastructure to the classification script so that agent runs surface on the os.agno.com dashboard.

The developer first checked whether the classification pipeline was already logging to agno and learned that traces go to OpenObserve via OpenTelemetry, but os.agno.com wasn't configured. They decided to enable it. Claude explained that `setup_agno_tracing()` was already wired into the server at startup but needed to be added to the standalone script to capture traces. The developer added the `setup_agno_tracing()` call to `run-classify-tier1.sh` so traces write to `~/.meridian/agno_traces.db`.

The developer then realized they needed session IDs to group runs on the dashboard — without them, each run appears isolated. They threaded `session_id` as an optional parameter through `_run_classification()`, `classify_tier1()`, and `stage_classify_tier1()` in `pipeline.py`, passing `f"classify-testing-{hour}"` so script runs are tagged distinctly from production pipeline runs. The changes compiled cleanly.

When testing the script against hour `2026-06-29T12`, the developer discovered that the MLX server was missing the `mlx_embeddings` module, which blocked the distiller step. They identified this by running `curl` directly to the `/distill_hour` endpoint and parsing the error response — the script was silently swallowing the module-not-found error and exiting with zero sessions. The fix is `pip install mlx-embeddings` in the services venv before retrying.

[08:10 · Claude Code]
The developer debugged and fixed three interconnected issues preventing the worklog pipeline from running properly.

First, the developer investigated why `/distill_hour` was returning 500 errors with "No module named 'mlx_embeddings'". They traced the root cause to a stale Python virtual environment—`mlx-embeddings` was declared as an optional dependency under `[mlx]` in `pyproject.toml` and present in `uv.lock`, but the venv had never been synced with those extras. The installation script (`install.sh` → `setup-services.sh`) correctly runs `uv sync --extra mlx --extra pm_worklog_update`, but the developer's local venv predated the addition of this package and hadn't been updated. They identified that the venv needed to be re-synced to match what new installations would get.

Second, they discovered a separate import failure in the worklog pipeline where `pipeline.py` was importing `WORKLOG_SYSTEM` from `agents.worklog_pipeline.worklog`, but that constant had been moved and renamed to `SYSTEM` in `agents.worklog_pipeline.prompts.worklog`. The developer fixed the import statement to pull from the correct location with the correct name.

Third, they found that `openinference-instrumentation-agno` was being imported at runtime but never declared as a dependency, causing startup warnings. Rather than skip this gracefully, they added it to the `pm_worklog_update` optional extra in `pyproject.toml` and regenerated `uv.lock` so it would be installed as part of the standard setup.

As a separate issue, the developer also fixed an OpenObserve authentication problem where the user database held a password hash that didn't match what was in `settings.json`. This happened because the DB was initialized on Jun 14 before the install script was updated on Jun 12 to source credentials from `settings.json`—the plist only seeds the password on first creation, so the two diverged. The developer stopped the OO service, deleted the stale user row, and restarted it to force OO to recreate the user from the current plist configuration.

candidates.title: Improve PM worklog update accuracy
candidates.doc: [Task] Improve PM worklog update accuracy. Epic: Accuracy & Automated Deep-Eval. Context: worklogs and observability already exist; this ticket is about raising the correctness of which tickets get matched and what the worklog text says, measured by an eval harness.
